questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman with a 9-year history of type 2 diabetes (on metformin and dapagliflozin) presents with 2 days of nausea, vomiting, and malaise during a febrile pyelonephritis. She is tachypneic with deep sighing breaths. Glucose 12.6 mmol/L, Na 134, Cl 96, HCO₃ 9, and serum ketones strongly positive. Which statement best explains her presentation?
options: Metformin has accumulated and produced a high-anion-gap lactic acidosis
Her SGLT2 inhibitor kept glucose near-normal while counter-regulatory hormones drove unchecked ketogenesis
The infection caused a non-anion-gap acidosis from bicarbonate loss
Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state from inadequate insulin secretion
questions.explain: This is euglycemic DKA, a hallmark risk of SGLT2 inhibitors during acute stress/infection. Anion gap = 134−(96+9)=29 with ketonemia, yet glucose is only 12.6 because ongoing glucosuria masks the hyperglycemia. The stress of pyelonephritis drove a counter-regulatory hormone surge (glucagon, cortisol, epinephrine) → lipolysis and ketogenesis.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with T2DM, HbA1c 8.6%, BMI 37, and Class II obesity wants a second-line agent that will also help him lose weight before knee surgery. eGFR 78. Which is most appropriate to add to metformin?
questions.explain: GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide) deliver potent A1c lowering plus substantial weight loss via central satiety effects — ideal when weight loss is the stated goal. Sulfonylureas (glyburide) cause weight gain and hypoglycemia; DPP-4 inhibitors and acarbose are weight-neutral with weaker A1c efficacy.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old with T1DM is found obtunded. Glucose 33 mmol/L, K⁺ 2.8 mmol/L, HCO₃ 6, pH 7.04. Normal saline is running. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Give an IV bolus of 10 units regular insulin immediately
Start an IV insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr
Administer IV potassium chloride and withhold insulin until K⁺ ≥ 3.3
Give IV sodium bicarbonate to correct the acidemia
questions.explain: Despite severe DKA, the K⁺ of 2.8 is critically low. Insulin drives K⁺ intracellularly and can precipitate fatal arrhythmia. Per the algorithm, if K⁺ < 3.3, hold insulin and replace potassium first; insulin begins only once K⁺ ≥ 3.3.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old with T2DM has a single random urine albumin-creatinine ratio (UACR) of 4.2 mg/mmol on a day she also has a febrile UTI. eGFR is normal. What is the most appropriate interpretation/next step?
options: Diagnose diabetic nephropathy and start an ACE inhibitor today
The result is diagnostic of overt nephropathy (UACR > 20)
Repeat the UACR after the UTI resolves; confirm across multiple samples before diagnosing
Normal eGFR rules out any nephropathy, so no further testing is needed
questions.explain: A single elevated UACR cannot diagnose nephropathy — transient causes (UTI, exercise, fever, hyperglycemia, CHF, menstruation) must be excluded. Microalbuminuria requires confirmation across repeated samples. Early diabetic nephropathy also features hyperfiltration that can keep eGFR normal/elevated.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old woman with Hashimoto's was started on levothyroxine 2 weeks ago. She now has profound fatigue, vomiting, dizziness, and hypotension, with hyperpigmentation of palmar creases and a recent scar. What process was precipitated, and what causes the pigmentation?
options: Myxedema coma; low metabolic rate causing carotenemia
Adrenal (Addisonian) crisis; elevated ACTH co-stimulating melanocortin receptors via shared POMC precursor
questions.explain: Starting levothyroxine in unrecognized primary adrenal insufficiency raises metabolic demand for cortisol that the failing adrenals can't meet → adrenal crisis. Low cortisol removes negative feedback → high ACTH, which shares the POMC precursor with MSH and stimulates melanocortin receptors → hyperpigmentation (scars, creases, buccal mucosa).

questions.stem: A 42-year-old has central obesity, purple striae, and proximal weakness. 24-h urine free cortisol is high, and an overnight 1-mg dexamethasone test fails to suppress. Plasma ACTH is 0.5 pg/mL (normal > 4). Where is the primary pathology?
questions.explain: Confirmed endogenous hypercortisolism with a suppressed (low) ACTH indicates an ACTH-independent source — autonomous cortisol production by an adrenal adenoma/carcinoma suppressing pituitary ACTH. High/normal ACTH would point to a pituitary or ectopic source.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old with episodic headache, palpitations, and diaphoresis has a 2.9-cm heterogeneous adrenal mass (40 HU) and markedly elevated 24-h urinary metanephrines. What is the correct pre-operative pharmacologic sequence?
options: Alpha-blocker (e.g., doxazosin) first, then add a beta-blocker
Start a beta-blocker and proceed directly to surgery
questions.explain: In pheochromocytoma, alpha blockade must precede beta blockade. Beta-blocking first leaves unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction → hypertensive crisis. Alpha-blockers (doxazosin/phenoxybenzamine) are started first, then a beta-blocker for rate control before resection.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman with 4 months of amenorrhea and new galactorrhea recently started metoclopramide for nausea. β-hCG negative, TSH 1.5, prolactin 190 µg/L (normal 4–23). What is the best next step?
questions.explain: Metoclopramide is a dopamine receptor antagonist that removes tonic inhibition of lactotrophs, causing hyperprolactinemia (often up to ~200 µg/L). With normal TSH and negative pregnancy test, the right move is to stop the offending drug and reassess before imaging or a dopamine agonist.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old woman has irregular menses, acne, and hirsutism with a normal BMI. Her physician wants to exclude an adrenal enzyme deficiency before labeling her PCOS. What is the best initial screening test?
questions.explain: A morning, follicular-phase 17-OH progesterone is the best screen for non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency), which mimics PCOS. A level > 6 nmol/L is then confirmed with an ACTH stimulation test.

questions.stem: A 195-cm, 29-year-old man with childhood learning difficulties, prior pubertal gynecomastia, small firm 3-cc testes, and azoospermia is evaluated for infertility. Which hormone profile is expected?
options: Low LH, low FSH, low testosterone
High LH, high FSH, low (or low-normal) testosterone
Low LH, high FSH, normal testosterone
questions.explain: This is classic Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY): primary (hypergonadotropic) hypogonadism from seminiferous tubule dysgenesis → loss of negative feedback → high LH, high FSH, low testosterone.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old transgender woman on transdermal estradiol and oral spironolactone reports dizziness and muscle weakness. Which lab abnormality is she at greatest risk for?
questions.explain: Spironolactone is a mineralocorticoid (aldosterone) receptor antagonist as well as an androgen blocker, so it spares potassium → risk of hyperkalemia; monitor K⁺ and creatinine. Hyperprolactinemia/hepatotoxicity are associated with cyproterone; polycythemia with testosterone therapy.

questions.stem: Three days after parathyroidectomy for long-standing primary hyperparathyroidism, a 61-year-old has perioral numbness, carpopedal spasm, a positive Chvostek sign, and prolonged QT. Calcium 1.72 mmol/L (albumin normal), phosphate 1.6, Mg 0.70. Best initial management?
options: Oral vitamin D and outpatient follow-up
IV calcium gluconate with cardiac monitoring
Observation — this is expected and benign
questions.explain: Hungry bone syndrome: after removing chronic PTH excess, high-turnover bone rapidly takes up calcium/phosphate → acute symptomatic hypocalcemia with tetany and prolonged QT. Treat with IV calcium gluconate + monitoring (and correct magnesium); bisphosphonates would worsen it.

questions.stem: A 77-year-old man has fatigue, constipation, polyuria, and confusion. Calcium 3.05 mmol/L, phosphate low-normal, PTH 13 pmol/L (elevated), 25-OH vitamin D normal. Best next step in acute management?
questions.explain: This is PTH-dependent hypercalcemia (likely primary hyperparathyroidism: high Ca, high/inappropriately-normal PTH, low-normal phosphate). Acute symptomatic hypercalcemia is first treated with IV isotonic saline for volume repletion and calciuresis. Surgery may be definitive later.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman has a 2.0-cm solid thyroid nodule with palpitations and heat intolerance. TSH is suppressed; ultrasound shows no microcalcifications or suspicious nodes. Next best investigation?
questions.explain: With a suppressed TSH, the next step is a radionuclide scan. A 'hot' (functioning) nodule is almost never malignant, so FNA can be avoided; FNA is the path when TSH is normal/high and ultrasound features warrant it.

questions.stem: A 79-year-old woman on denosumab every 6 months for osteoporosis missed her dose and is now 4 months overdue. She feels well and wants to simply stop. What is the key concern?
options: Denosumab persists in bone for decades, so no concern
Rebound increase in bone turnover with risk of multiple vertebral fractures
questions.explain: Unlike bisphosphonates, denosumab has no prolonged skeletal retention. Stopping or delaying it triggers a rebound in bone turnover with risk of multiple vertebral fractures; patients must be redosed promptly or transitioned to a bisphosphonate.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is referred by his dentist for a widening jaw (prognathism). He notes his shoe size and ring size have increased, and he was recently diagnosed with type 2 diabetes and carpal tunnel syndrome. What is the most appropriate initial screening test?
questions.explain: The picture is acromegaly (GH excess). Because GH is pulsatile, the initial screen is an IGF-1 level; confirmation is an oral glucose suppression test (failure of GH to suppress). MRI follows biochemical confirmation.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman who is 8 weeks pregnant has palpitations, heat intolerance, weight loss, a diffuse goiter, suppressed TSH, elevated free T4, and positive TRAb. What is the preferred treatment, and which severe side effect must she be warned about?
questions.explain: This is Graves disease in the first trimester. PTU is preferred early in pregnancy because methimazole is teratogenic in the first trimester. Warn about agranulocytosis — seek urgent care for fever/sore throat. Radioactive iodine is contraindicated in pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman has refractory hypertension (180/110) and a potassium of 2.4 mmol/L. Workup shows low plasma renin and high serum aldosterone. You choose a diuretic that directly blocks the sodium channel in the collecting duct. Which agent?
questions.explain: This is primary aldosteronism (Conn's): high aldosterone, low renin, hypokalemia, refractory HTN. Amiloride is the ENaC sodium-channel blocker. (Spironolactone/eplerenone are also appropriate but act as aldosterone-receptor antagonists, not ENaC blockers.)

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with T2DM (metformin + sitagliptin) is admitted for an infected foot ulcer. On day 2, eGFR falls to 24 mL/min/1.73m² (baseline 52) and random glucose is 16.4 mmol/L. Best medication adjustment?
options: Stop metformin, stop sitagliptin, start subcutaneous basal insulin
Continue metformin, switch sitagliptin to canagliflozin
Continue both unchanged and recheck in a week
questions.explain: Metformin is contraindicated at eGFR < 30 (lactic acidosis risk) and sitagliptin needs dose reduction. With severe infection and marked hyperglycemia, oral agents are inadequate — insulin is the safest, most titratable choice in acute renal impairment plus infection.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with T2DM and stable ischemic heart disease has total cholesterol 6.2, triglycerides 1.8, HDL 0.9, LDL 3.1 mmol/L. A1c and BP are at target. Most appropriate initial step for his lipids?
questions.explain: Diabetics ≥40 or with established vascular disease require a statin. The validated target is LDL < 2.0 mmol/L (or ≥50% reduction). Fibrates are for severe hypertriglyceridemia; ezetimibe is add-on, not first-line.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman with disruptive hot flashes and night sweats has diet-controlled hypertension, an intact uterus, and no history of breast cancer or VTE. She wants hormone therapy. Most appropriate regimen?
questions.explain: With an intact uterus, a progestogen must accompany estrogen to prevent endometrial hyperplasia. Transdermal estrogen is preferred given hypertension (bypasses hepatic first-pass, avoiding clotting-factor effects). She's a candidate (symptomatic, < 60).

questions.stem: A newborn with a 46,XY karyotype has bilateral undescended testes, normal epididymides/vasa deferentia/seminal vesicles, but predominantly female external genitalia with a blind vaginal pouch and clitoromegaly. Which enzyme deficiency explains this?
questions.explain: Internal male (Wolffian) structures present + absent Müllerian structures means testosterone and MIF are working. Female-appearing external genitalia means DHT was not made — a 5-alpha-reductase deficiency (testosterone→DHT), which is required for male external genital development.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old woman with femoral-neck T-score −1.4 and lumbar T-score −1.8 develops back pain; spine X-ray shows a new T12 compression fracture after bending to lift laundry. Basic labs are normal. Best interpretation and additional test?
options: Clinically significant osteoporosis; consider SPEP to screen for myeloma
Primary hyperparathyroidism; order a parathyroid scan
Normal age-related loss; repeat DXA in 10 years
questions.explain: A low-trauma vertebral fracture defines clinically significant osteoporosis and high fracture risk regardless of an osteopenic T-score — pharmacologic therapy is warranted. SPEP is considered because vertebral fractures can herald multiple myeloma.

cards.prompt: A woman has thyrotoxic symptoms, suppressed TSH, high free T4, and a painless gland. Radioactive iodine uptake is low. Best treatment?
cards.explain: Low uptake = destructive thyroiditis — hormone is leaking, not being overproduced — so thionamides do nothing. Treat symptoms with a beta-blocker and wait it out.

cards.prompt: Put thyroid-storm treatment in the correct order:
items: Iodine — at least 1 h AFTER PTU
cards.explain: Iodine must come after PTU, or it fuels synthesis (Jod-Basedow). Order: β-blocker → PTU → iodine (≥1 h later) → steroids.

cards.text: In primary hypothyroidism, TSH is {{high}} and free T4 is {{low}}. Start levothyroxine, but first screen for {{adrenal insufficiency}} — replacing thyroxine alone can precipitate an adrenal crisis.
cards.explain: Thyroxine raises metabolic demand for cortisol; if the adrenals are failing too, you trigger a crisis. Cortisol before thyroxine.

cards.prompt: Match each test to what it signals:
cards.explain: Thyroglobulin is a post-thyroidectomy surveillance marker for papillary/follicular cancer; calcitonin tracks medullary (C-cell) cancer.

cards.prompt: High calcium, high/inappropriately-normal PTH, low phosphate. Acutely symptomatic. First step?
cards.explain: This is primary hyperparathyroidism. Acute symptomatic hypercalcemia → IV saline first (volume + calciuresis); calcitonin/bisphosphonate follow; surgery is definitive later.

cards.prompt: Which findings favour FHH over primary hyperparathyroidism? (select all)
cards.explain: FHH (CaSR mutation) gives low urinary calcium (CCCR < 0.01) and a benign family history — surgery does not help. Stones/bone disease point to true primary hyperPTH.

cards.text: Tetany three days after parathyroidectomy = {{hungry bone syndrome}}. Treat with IV {{calcium gluconate}} and always replace {{magnesium}}, which keeps hypocalcemia refractory.
cards.explain: After chronic PTH excess is removed, high-turnover bone rapidly takes up calcium → acute hypocalcemia.

cards.prompt: Confirmed hypercortisolism. Plasma ACTH is suppressed (low). Where is the lesion?
cards.explain: Low ACTH = ACTH-independent → autonomous adrenal cortisol production suppressing pituitary ACTH. High/normal ACTH would mean pituitary or ectopic.

cards.prompt: Why are patients with primary adrenal insufficiency hyperpigmented, while those with secondary disease are not?
cards.answer: Low cortisol removes negative feedback → high ACTH. ACTH is cleaved from POMC, which also yields MSH; the excess stimulates melanocortin receptors → hyperpigmentation (creases, scars, buccal mucosa). In secondary insufficiency ACTH is low, so there is no pigmentation (and aldosterone is preserved → no hyperkalemia).
cards.explain: POMC is the shared precursor — the link to remember for the pigmentation finding.

cards.prompt: Order the pre-operative management of a pheochromocytoma:
cards.explain: Alpha before beta — beta-blocking first leaves unopposed alpha vasoconstriction → hypertensive crisis.

cards.prompt: A patient on an SGLT2 inhibitor is acidotic and ketotic during sepsis, but glucose is only 12 mmol/L. Diagnosis?
cards.explain: Euglycemic DKA — ongoing glucosuria masks the hyperglycemia while stress hormones drive ketogenesis. Check ketones/gap in any acidotic patient on a flozin.

items: Add dextrose when glucose ~14, continue insulin to gap closure
cards.explain: Fluids first; potassium before/with insulin (insulin drives K⁺ into cells); dextrose lets you keep insulin running until the anion gap closes.

cards.prompt: Match each diabetes drug to its key caution/benefit:
cards.explain: These four mappings cover most diabetes drug-choice questions.

cards.prompt: Coarse features, enlarging jaw/hands, new diabetes and carpal tunnel. Best initial screening test?
cards.explain: GH is pulsatile, so screen with IGF-1; confirm with an OGTT (GH fails to suppress), then MRI.

cards.text: PCOS is diagnosed by Rotterdam {{2 of 3}} criteria and is a diagnosis of exclusion — rule out non-classic CAH with a morning {{17-OH progesterone}}. For ovulation induction, first-line is {{letrozole}}.
cards.explain: The other Rotterdam exclusions: prolactin, TSH, and Cushing screening if features fit.

cards.prompt: Which features suggest an androgen-secreting tumour rather than PCOS? (select all)
cards.explain: Rapid virilization with very high testosterone (ovarian) or very high DHEAS (adrenal) → image, don't label PCOS. Slow, mild hirsutism fits PCOS.

guides.title: Topic 01 Thyroid detailed
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 01 ◇

Thyroid

spans Endo II + Endo IV · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
Physiology you must have to reason through the rest
Axis: hypothalamic TRH → pituitary TSH → thyroid T4 (& some T3). T4/T3 feed back to suppress TSH. Negative feedback is why TSH is the most sensitive screen.
T4 = prohormone; T3 = active. Peripheral 5′-deiodinase converts T4→T3 (mostly liver/kidney). Illness shunts to inactive reverse T3.
Synthesis: iodide trapped → organified onto thyroglobulin by thyroid peroxidase (TPO) → coupling. Thionamides block TPO; PTU also blocks peripheral T4→T3.
Wolff–Chaikoff: a large iodine load transiently shuts off synthesis (autoregulation) → basis for giving iodine in storm + amiodarone hypothyroidism.
Jod–Basedow: iodine load triggers hyperthyroidism in an autonomous nodular gland → why iodine is given after a thionamide in storm.
Binding proteins (TBG): estrogen/pregnancy ↑TBG → ↑total T4 but free normal; nephrotic syndrome/androgens ↓TBG. Always interpret free hormone.
★ Reading thyroid function tests — TSH is the screen
StateTSHFree T4Read it as / action
Primary hypothyroid↑↓gland failing; feedback drives TSH up → levothyroxine
Subclinical hypothyroid↑normaltreat if TSH>10, symptomatic, pregnant/planning, or TPO+ with goiter
Primary hyperthyroid↓↑check fT3 too (T3-predominant toxicosis); then RAIU
Subclinical hyperthyroid↓normaltreat if TSH<0.1 & age>65 / AF / osteoporosis risk
Central (secondary)↓ or inappropriately normal↓pituitary/hypothalamic — follow free T4, not TSH; screen other axes
Sick-euthyroidlow-normal↓T3 first; ↑rT3non-thyroidal illness — don't test or treat in acute illness
↑TSH and ↑fT4↑↑TSH-secreting adenoma, resistance, or biotin assay interference
Antibodies & markers — what each one tells you
TestMeans
TRAb / TSIstimulating TSH-receptor antibody → Graves; predicts neonatal Graves; tracks activity
Anti-TPOHashimoto & postpartum/silent thyroiditis; predicts progression of subclinical disease
Anti-thyroglobulinHashimoto; also interferes with thyroglobulin surveillance assay in cancer follow-up
Thyroglobulinsurveillance marker after total thyroidectomy for differentiated cancer (should be undetectable)
Calcitonin (±CEA)marker for medullary carcinoma (parafollicular C cells)
★ Thyroid emergencies
Thyroid storm — fever, AF/SVT, agitation→delirium, vomiting/diarrhea, heart failure; precipitated by infection, surgery, trauma, iodine/contrast, RAI, or stopping thionamides. Dx is clinical (Burch–Wartofsky). Treatment order matters:
① β-blocker (propranolol — also blunts T4→T3) ② PTU (blocks synthesis + peripheral conversion) ③ iodine (Lugol/SSKI) ≥1 h AFTER PTU (give synthesis-block first, then block release) ④ hydrocortisone (blocks conversion, covers relative adrenal insufficiency) ⑤ cooling, fluids, treat trigger.
Myxedema coma — decompensated hypothyroidism: hypothermia, ↓LOC/coma, hypoventilation (CO₂ retention), bradycardia, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia; often elderly woman + cold/infection trigger. Treatment: IV levothyroxine (± T3) + IV hydrocortisone FIRST (cover concurrent adrenal insufficiency before thyroid hormone) + passive rewarming + ventilatory/fluid support + treat trigger. High mortality.

Hypothyroidism

Primary causes Hashimoto (anti-TPO) #1 in iodine-replete areas; iodine deficiency #1 worldwide; post-RAI/thyroidectomy; drugs (amiodarone, lithium, checkpoint inhibitors); subacute thyroiditis phase
Central pituitary/hypothalamic disease; ↓TSH + ↓fT4 — monitor by free T4
Clinical
Classic fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, constipation, dry skin, hair loss, bradycardia, delayed-relaxation reflexes, menorrhagia
Easy to miss hyponatremia, ↑LDL, ↑CK/myalgia, macrocytic anemia, carpal tunnel, hoarseness, pericardial/pleural effusions, non-pitting (myxedema) edema
Treatment
Levothyroxine ~1.6 µg/kg/day; elderly/CAD start low (25–50 µg, titrate) to avoid angina/arrhythmia
Administration empty stomach, 30–60 min before food; separate from Ca, iron, PPI, coffee (↓absorption)
↑ requirement pregnancy, estrogen, malabsorption, rifampin/phenytoin/carbamazepine
⚠ Before starting screen for adrenal insufficiency — levothyroxine alone can precipitate adrenal crisis
Monitor TSH at 6–8 wk (central: free T4)

Hyperthyroidism / Thyrotoxicosis

Classic weight loss + ↑appetite, heat intolerance, palpitations/AF, fine tremor, anxiety, diarrhea, brisk reflexes, sweating, osteoporosis, proximal myopathy
Apathetic (elderly) may present only with AF, weight loss, depression, failure to thrive
The pivot: radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU)
High & diffuse Graves disease
Single hot focus toxic adenoma
Multiple patchy hot foci toxic multinodular goiter
LOW / absent uptake destructive thyroiditis, exogenous/factitious intake, iodine load, struma ovarii (ectopic) — hormone released, not synthesized
Graves specifics
Confirm TRAb/TSI positive; diffuse goiter ± bruit
Extrathyroidal orbitopathy (proptosis/diplopia — worse with smoking, high TRAb, and RAI), pretibial myxedema, thyroid acropachy

Hyperthyroidism — treatment options

OptionKey points / cautions
β-blockersymptom control immediately; propranolol also blunts T4→T3. Bridge for all causes incl. thyroiditis.
Methimazolefirst-line thionamide (Graves): once-daily, potent. Teratogenic 1st trimester (aplasia cutis, embryopathy). SE: agranulocytosis, cholestatic hepatitis, rash.
PTUblocks peripheral conversion → preferred in storm & 1st trimester. Higher (fulminant) hepatotoxicity — black box; 2–3× daily.
Radioactive iodinedefinitive; causes hypothyroidism. Contraindicated in pregnancy/breastfeeding; can worsen orbitopathy (cover with steroids).
Surgeryfor large/compressive goiter, suspected malignancy, severe orbitopathy, or drug-intolerant pregnancy. Risks: hypoparathyroidism, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury.
Before any thionamide baseline CBC + LFTs; warn: stop & check CBC if fever/sore throat (agranulocytosis, usually first 90 days)

Thyroiditis

All destructive forms LOW uptake; often triphasic: thyrotoxic → hypothyroid → euthyroid; thyrotoxic phase treated with β-blocker ONLY (no thionamides)
Subacute granulomatous (de Quervain) PAINFUL tender goiter, post-viral, ↑↑ESR/CRP → NSAIDs, steroids if severe
Silent / postpartum PAINLESS, autoimmune (anti-TPO+), within 12 mo of delivery; ~20–50% → permanent hypothyroid; recurs in future pregnancies; annual TSH
Hashimoto chronic lymphocytic; anti-TPO; ends hypothyroid (early transient "hashitoxicosis"); risk of thyroid lymphoma
Drug-induced amiodarone, lithium, interferon, immune checkpoint inhibitors
Riedel woody-hard fixed gland; IgG4-related fibrosis

Nodule & thyroid cancer

Work-up — TSH directs the next test
Suppressed TSH → radionuclide scan; a hot (functioning) nodule is almost never malignant → treat the hyperthyroidism, no FNA
Normal / high TSH → ultrasound; FNA based on size + sonographic risk (microcalcifications, hypoechoic, irregular margins, taller-than-wide, abnormal cervical nodes). Cytology reported by Bethesda category.
Cancer types
TypeFeatures
Papillarymost common; lymphatic spread; Orphan-Annie nuclei, psammoma bodies, BRAF; excellent prognosis; prior radiation risk
Follicularhematogenous (lung/bone); needs histology for capsular/vascular invasion — FNA cannot distinguish from adenoma; Hürthle-cell variant
Medullaryparafollicular C cells → calcitonin(+CEA), amyloid stroma; MEN2 / RET — rule out pheochromocytoma before surgery, check for hyperparathyroidism
Anaplasticelderly, rapidly enlarging, airway compromise; very poor prognosis
Lymphomaarises on a background of Hashimoto
Differentiated cancer Rx/surveillance thyroidectomy ± RAI ablation; TSH-suppressive levothyroxine; follow thyroglobulin + neck US (anti-Tg antibodies can confound the assay)

Special situations & pitfalls

Amiodarone (≈40% iodine by weight)
Hypothyroidism (AIH) common, via Wolff-Chaikoff → levothyroxine; usually continue amiodarone
AIT type 1 iodine-induced on nodular/Graves gland (↑uptake/vascularity) → thionamide ± perchlorate
AIT type 2 destructive thyroiditis (↓uptake) → glucocorticoids
Pregnancy
hCG mimics TSH gestational thyrotoxicosis / hyperemesis gravidarum — self-limited, no antithyroid drug
Antithyroid PTU 1st trimester → switch to methimazole 2nd/3rd; RAI contraindicated
Hypothyroid levothyroxine need rises early — increase dose ~30%, trimester-specific TSH targets
Assay & lab traps
Biotin supplements interfere with immunoassays → falsely ↓TSH + ↑T4 (mimics Graves); hold biotin before testing
TBG shifts estrogen/pregnancy ↑total T4 (free normal); read free hormone
Don't screen in acute illness sick-euthyroid changes resolve with recovery
Topic 01 / Thyroid (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 02 Calcium and Bone
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 02 ◇

Calcium & Bone

Endo IV · links to Renal CKD-MBD · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
Physiology — the two hormones that move calcium
PTH (parathyroids, sensed by the calcium-sensing receptor, CaSR): released when Ca falls. Raises Ca, lowers phosphate. ↑bone resorption, ↑distal renal Ca reabsorption, ↓renal phosphate reabsorption (phosphaturic), ↑renal 1-α-hydroxylase.
Vitamin D: skin/diet → liver 25-OH (storage form, the one you measure) → kidney 1-α-hydroxylase → 1,25-(OH)₂D (calcitriol, active). Raises both Ca and phosphate via gut absorption.
Quick rule: PTH splits Ca and phosphate in opposite directions; vitamin D moves them together. Reading phosphate alongside Ca tells you which hormone is at fault.
FGF23 (from bone): phosphaturic + suppresses 1,25-vit D — the early driver in CKD-MBD. Calcitonin lowers Ca but is minor in humans.
Corrected Ca: ~45% of total Ca is albumin-bound. Corrected Ca = measured + 0.02 × (40 − albumin g/L). Low albumin → low total but normal ionized (pseudohypocalcemia). When unsure, measure ionized Ca.
pH effect: alkalosis ↑albumin binding → ↓ionized Ca → tetany (e.g., hyperventilation), even when total Ca is normal.
★ Pattern recognition — always check PTH first when Ca is abnormal
DiagnosisCaPTHPO₄Clue
Primary hyperparathyroidism↑↑ / inappropriately normal↓PTH-driven; ↑/normal urine Ca; adenoma 85%
FHH↑↑ / normal±CaSR mutation; LOW urine Ca (CCCR <0.01) — do NOT operate
Malignancy (PTHrP)↑↓ (suppressed)↓PTHrP mimics PTH; squamous/renal/breast
Vit D excess / granulomatous↑↓↑sarcoid/lymphoma make 1,25-D; ↑25-D in toxicity
Hypoparathyroidism↓↓↑post-surgical #1; ↑phosphate
Vitamin D deficiency↓ / low-normal↑ (2°)↓↓25-OH D; the commonest cause of ↑PTH
2° hyperPTH (CKD)↓ / normal↑↑↓1,25-D + phosphate retention
3° hyperPTH↑↑↑↑autonomous glands after long-standing CKD
Pseudohypoparathyroidism↓↑ (resistance)↑Albright phenotype; end-organ PTH resistance
★ Calcium emergencies
Severe / symptomatic hypercalcemia (Ca >3.5 mmol/L, or confusion/arrhythmia): ① IV isotonic saline first — volume repletion + calciuresis ② calcitonin (fast onset, tachyphylaxis in 48 h) ③ IV bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid — onset 2–4 d, durable) ④ denosumab if renal failure/refractory ⑤ treat the cause; dialysis if severe + renal failure. Avoid thiazides (raise Ca); add loop diuretic only once volume-replete/overloaded.
Severe hypocalcemia / tetany (Chvostek, Trousseau, laryngospasm, seizures, prolonged QT): IV calcium gluconate with cardiac monitoring → then oral Ca + active vitamin D (calcitriol). Always check & replace magnesium — hypomagnesemia causes refractory hypocalcemia by impairing PTH secretion/action. Classic setting: post-parathyroidectomy hungry-bone syndrome.

Hypercalcemia

Symptoms "stones, bones, groans, psychiatric moans" — kidney stones/nephrogenic DI/polyuria, bone pain, constipation/abdominal pain/pancreatitis/PUD, confusion/depression; short QT
Causes — 90% are two
Primary hyperPTH commonest in outpatients (often asymptomatic)
Malignancy commonest in inpatients; PTHrP, osteolysis, or 1,25-D (lymphoma)
Others vit D toxicity, granulomatous (sarcoid), thiazides, lithium, milk-alkali, immobilization, thyrotoxicosis, FHH
Work-up
Check PTH first ↑/normal = PTH-mediated (primary, FHH, tertiary); ↓ = PTH-independent (malignancy, vit D)

Hypocalcemia

Symptoms perioral/digital paresthesias, cramps, Chvostek & Trousseau, tetany, laryngospasm, seizures; prolonged QT
Causes
Hypoparathyroidism post-thyroid/parathyroid surgery #1, autoimmune, DiGeorge
Vitamin D deficiency / CKD ↓absorption / ↓1,25-D
Hypomagnesemia functional hypoPTH — refractory until Mg replaced
↑Phosphate tumor lysis, rhabdomyolysis
Other pancreatitis, hungry bone, citrate (massive transfusion), sepsis, pseudohypoparathyroidism
Work-up corrected/ionized Ca, Mg, phosphate, PTH, 25-OH D, creatinine

Hyperparathyroidism — the three types

Primary adenoma 85% (hyperplasia, rarely carcinoma); ↑Ca ↑PTH ↓phosphate. Complications: osteoporosis (cortical/distal radius), nephrolithiasis, osteitis fibrosa cystica / brown tumors
Parathyroidectomy — when (asymptomatic 1°)
Operate if Ca >0.25 mmol/L above ULN, age <50, eGFR <60, T-score ≤−2.5 or fragility fx, nephrolithiasis/marked hypercalciuria
Localize sestamibi + neck US pre-op; watch post-op hungry bone
Secondary appropriate ↑PTH from CKD (↑phosphate) or vit D deficiency (↓phosphate) — treat the cause
Tertiary autonomous glands after prolonged CKD → ↑Ca + ↑↑PTH → cinacalcet or parathyroidectomy
⚠ Don't miss FHH mimics primary but low urine Ca (CCCR <0.01) — surgery won't help

Osteoporosis

Define (DXA T-score) ≤−2.5 osteoporosis; −1 to −2.4 osteopenia; any fragility fracture = osteoporosis regardless of T-score; use Z-score if young/premenopausal
Risk factors age, postmenopausal/estrogen loss, glucocorticoids, smoking, alcohol, low BMI, prior fx, hyperthyroid/hyperPTH, hypogonadism, malabsorption, RA
Secondary work-up Ca, phosphate, PTH, 25-OH D, TSH, testosterone (men), SPEP (myeloma), celiac serology, 24-h urine Ca
Risk tool FRAX 10-yr fracture probability guides borderline cases
Treatment
Treat if T ≤−2.5, hip/vertebral fragility fx, or high FRAX. Ensure Ca + vit D, weight-bearing exercise, fall prevention
Bisphosphonate (1st-line) oral upright 30–60 min empty stomach (or IV zoledronic acid yearly); SE: esophagitis, ONJ, atypical femoral fx, hypocalcemia (replete vit D/Ca first), IV acute-phase reaction
Denosumab RANKL inhibitor, SC q6mo; OK in renal impairment; rebound vertebral fx if stopped/delayed — never just discontinue
Anabolic teriparatide/abaloparatide (severe; avoid if prior radiation/Paget); romosozumab (CV caution) — follow with an antiresorptive
Raloxifene (SERM) vertebral only; ↓breast cancer but ↑VTE
Glucocorticoid-induced prophylax with bisphosphonate if chronic steroids

Paget disease of bone

Mechanism disordered remodeling — excessive osteoclast resorption then chaotic osteoblast formation → enlarged, weak, deformed bone
Presentation often asymptomatic, found on isolated ↑ALP; bone pain, bowing, skull enlargement/frontal bossing, hearing loss (CN VIII), fractures, high-output heart failure; rare osteosarcoma
Labs ↑↑ALP with normal Ca and phosphate; ↑urinary hydroxyproline
Imaging mixed lytic + sclerotic, "cotton-wool" skull; bone scan maps extent
Treatment symptomatic → IV zoledronic acid; track response with ALP

CKD-MBD & exam pitfalls

CKD mineral-bone disorder
Mechanism ↓1-α-hydroxylase → ↓1,25-D; phosphate retention; ↑FGF23 → ↓Ca → secondary hyperPTH
Pattern ↓Ca, ↑phosphate, ↑PTH, ↓1,25-vit D
Treat dietary phosphate restriction + phosphate binders (non-calcium e.g. sevelamer), active vit D (calcitriol), calcimimetic (cinacalcet) lowers PTH & Ca → renal osteodystrophy prevention
Pitfalls
Correct for albumin or measure ionized Ca before calling hypo-/hypercalcemia
Alkalosis tetany from ↓ionized Ca despite normal total (hyperventilation)
Replace Mg hypocalcemia stays refractory until magnesium corrected
Diuretics thiazides ↑Ca (can unmask hyperPTH); loops ↓Ca
Topic 02 / Calcium & Bone (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 03 Adrenal
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 03 ◇

Adrenal

Endo II (+ CAH from Endo III) · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
Physiology — "salt, sugar, sex; the deeper you go, the sweeter it gets"
Cortex zones (outer→inner): Glomerulosa → aldosterone (mineralocorticoid); Fasciculata → cortisol (glucocorticoid); Reticularis → androgens (DHEA). Medulla → catecholamines (chromaffin cells).
HPA axis: CRH → ACTH (cleaved from POMC) → cortisol; cortisol feeds back to suppress. Diurnal: peak AM, trough midnight (basis of late-night salivary test).
POMC link: high ACTH co-produces MSH → hyperpigmentation (primary adrenal insufficiency, ectopic ACTH, Nelson).
RAAS: renin (JG cells sense ↓BP/Na) → AngI →(ACE)→ AngII → vasoconstriction + aldosterone. Aldosterone acts on collecting-duct principal cells (ENaC): ↑Na in, ↑K & H⁺ out → excess = HTN + ↓K + metabolic alkalosis.
Aldosterone control = RAAS + K⁺, NOT ACTH — why it's preserved in secondary adrenal insufficiency.
Cortisol does: gluconeogenesis/insulin resistance, catabolism, immunosuppression, permissive for catecholamine vascular tone (why its loss → refractory shock), ↑appetite.
★ Localizing the lesion — screen to confirm, then ACTH/renin to localize
DiagnosisCortisol/AldoACTH / ReninElectrolytes & clue
Cushing — pituitary (disease)↑ cortisolACTH ↑/normalsuppresses on high-dose dex; MRI
Cushing — ectopic ACTH↑↑ cortisolACTH ↑↑marked ↓K alkalosis, rapid, pigmented; SCLC/carcinoid
Cushing — adrenal / exogenous↑ cortisolACTH ↓adrenal mass; exogenous steroids = #1 cause overall
Primary adrenal insuff. (Addison)↓ cortisol & aldoACTH ↑↓Na, ↑K, hyperpigmented; cosyntropin fails to rise
Secondary adrenal insuff.↓ cortisolACTH ↓aldo preserved → no ↑K, no pigment; steroid withdrawal
Primary aldosteronism (Conn)↑ aldorenin ↓ (↑ARR)HTN + ↓K + alkalosis
Pheochromocytoma↑ metanephrines—episodic HTN/headache/palpitations/sweats
CAH (21-OH deficiency)↓ cortisol ± aldoACTH ↑↑17-OHP; salt-wasting (↓Na ↑K) or virilizing
★ Adrenal emergencies
Adrenal (Addisonian) crisis — hypotension/shock refractory to fluids, ↓Na, ↑K, hypoglycemia, abdominal pain, fever. Triggers: infection, surgery, abrupt steroid withdrawal, starting levothyroxine in unrecognized AI. Tx: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg + aggressive isotonic saline (+ dextrose) + treat trigger. If undiagnosed, give dexamethasone (doesn't interfere with the cosyntropin test) and draw cortisol/ACTH — but never delay steroids to test.
Pheochromocytoma / catecholamine crisis — severe paroxysmal HTN, arrhythmia, end-organ ischemia. Never give a β-blocker first (unopposed α → crisis). Acute control with phentolamine or nitroprusside; definitive prep is α-blockade then β-blockade then resection.

Cushing syndrome

Features central obesity, moon face, buffalo hump, wide purple striae, proximal myopathy, easy bruising, HTN, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, ↓K alkalosis, mood change
#1 cause overall exogenous glucocorticoids (ask about steroids; ACTH suppressed, no adrenal mass)
Two-step work-up
① Confirm excess (2 of 3) 24-h urine free cortisol · late-night salivary cortisol · overnight 1-mg dexamethasone (fails to suppress)
② Localize with ACTH low → adrenal CT (adenoma/carcinoma) · high/normal → ACTH-dependent → pituitary MRI, high-dose dex (pituitary suppresses, ectopic doesn't), ± inferior petrosal sinus sampling
Treatment surgery (transsphenoidal / adrenalectomy / resect ectopic); steroidogenesis inhibitors (ketoconazole, metyrapone, osilodrostat) as bridge
Nelson syndrome pituitary corticotroph tumor enlargement after bilateral adrenalectomy

Adrenal insufficiency

Primary (Addison) cortex destroyed → ↓cortisol + ↓aldosterone + ↓androgens; ↑ACTH → hyperpigmentation; ↓Na, ↑K, hypoglycemia
Primary causes autoimmune (#1 developed world, ± APS), TB (#1 worldwide), adrenal hemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen), metastases, adrenoleukodystrophy
Secondary ↓ACTH (pituitary) / tertiary ↓CRH (chronic steroid withdrawal = commonest); aldosterone intact → no ↑K, no pigment; mild ↓Na possible
Diagnosis & treatment
Diagnose low AM cortisol; ACTH (↑ primary, ↓ secondary); cosyntropin (ACTH) stim — no cortisol rise = primary
Replace hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone (mineralocorticoid — primary only)
Sick-day rules double/triple glucocorticoid in illness; injectable for vomiting; medical-alert ID
⚠ don't start levothyroxine before glucocorticoid — precipitates crisis

Primary aldosteronism (Conn)

Why it matters commonest cause of secondary HTN; bilateral adrenal hyperplasia or unilateral adenoma
Suspect resistant HTN, HTN + hypokalemia (many are normokalemic), HTN + adrenal incidentaloma, young HTN
Labs HTN, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis
Screen aldosterone:renin ratio (↑aldo, suppressed renin)
Confirm saline-suppression/salt-loading (aldo fails to suppress)
Lateralize adrenal vein sampling: unilateral adenoma → adrenalectomy; bilateral hyperplasia → MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone)

Pheochromocytoma

What catecholamine-secreting medullary tumor (chromaffin cells); paraganglioma if extra-adrenal
Classic triad + HTN episodic headache, palpitations, diaphoresis; sustained or paroxysmal hypertension, pallor, anxiety
"Rule of 10s" ~10% bilateral, extra-adrenal, malignant; familial fraction is high
Genetics MEN2 (RET), VHL, NF1, SDHx — screen young/familial cases
Diagnose plasma free metanephrines or 24-h urine fractionated metanephrines → then CT/MRI (± MIBG)
Manage α-block first (phenoxybenzamine/doxazosin) ×10–14 d + volume expansion → then β-block → resection

Congenital adrenal hyperplasia

21-hydroxylase deficiency (90%) can't make cortisol/aldosterone → ACTH drive shunts precursors to androgens; screen with ↑17-OHP
Classic salt-wasting neonatal crisis: ↓Na, ↑K, hypotension; ambiguous genitalia in 46,XX
Non-classic (late-onset) hirsutism, acne, oligomenorrhea — PCOS mimic; morning 17-OHP → ACTH-stim to confirm
11β-hydroxylase def HTN (deoxycorticosterone) + virilization
17α-hydroxylase def HTN + ↓K, no androgens (absent secondary sexual development)
Treat glucocorticoid (suppress ACTH) ± mineralocorticoid (salt-wasting)

Incidentaloma & exam pitfalls

Adrenal incidentaloma
Two questions functioning? (1-mg dex for cortisol, plasma metanephrines, ARR if HTN/↓K) and malignant? (size >4 cm, >10 HU non-contrast, irregular, growth)
Most are non-functioning benign adenomas → observe; resect if functioning or suspicious/>4 cm
Pitfalls
Exclude pheo first before ANY adrenal surgery or biopsy
α before β in pheochromocytoma, always
Secondary AI no hyperkalemia, no hyperpigmentation (aldosterone preserved)
Treat crisis before testing dexamethasone preserves the cosyntropin test
Steroid taper chronic glucocorticoids suppress HPA — never stop abruptly
Topic 03 / Adrenal (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 04 Diabetes
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 04 ◇

Diabetes Mellitus

Endo I · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
Physiology & classification
Insulin (β-cell): anabolic — drives glucose into muscle/fat, suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis & lipolysis/ketogenesis. Glucagon (α-cell) + cortisol/epi/GH are counter-regulatory.
Type 1: autoimmune β-cell destruction → absolute insulinopenia (anti-GAD/IA-2); ketosis-prone; needs insulin. LADA = slow autoimmune in adults.
Type 2: insulin resistance + relative deficiency; obesity/metabolic syndrome; ketosis-resistant (usually). MODY = monogenic, young, non-obese, autosomal dominant.
Incretins (GLP-1, GIP) from gut amplify glucose-dependent insulin release — the target of GLP-1 RAs and DPP-4i. Secondary DM: steroids, Cushing, acromegaly, pancreatitis, thiazides, antipsychotics.
★ Diagnosis & glycemic targets
TestDiabetesPrediabetes
Fasting plasma glucose≥7.0 mmol/L6.1–6.9 (IFG)
HbA1c≥6.5%6.0–6.4
2-h 75 g OGTT≥11.1 mmol/L7.8–11.0 (IGT)
Random glucose≥11.1 + symptoms—
Confirm repeat/second abnormal test unless unequivocal hyperglycemia with symptoms
A1c target ≤7% most adults; ≤6.5% selected (low hypo risk); ≤8% frail/elderly/limited life expectancy
A1c falsely LOW hemolysis, sickle/thal, recent transfusion, pregnancy, EPO
A1c falsely HIGH iron/B12/folate deficiency, splenectomy, uremia, alcohol
★ Hyperglycemic emergencies & hypoglycemia
DKA (T1>T2) — glucose usually >14, pH <7.3, HCO₃ <18, ↑anion gap, ketones+. Precipitants: infection, non-adherence, new dx, MI, SGLT2 (euglycemic). Mgmt: ① isotonic saline ② K⁺ — hold insulin if K<3.3, add K when 3.3–5.3 ③ insulin 0.1 U/kg/h ④ add dextrose at glucose ~14, continue insulin until gap closes ⑤ overlap SC insulin before stopping drip.
HHS (T2, elderly) — glucose >33, osm >320, minimal ketones, pH >7.3, profound dehydration + altered mentation; higher mortality. Mgmt: aggressive fluids are the mainstay, cautious K⁺ + insulin, find trigger; watch thrombosis.
Hypoglycemia (<4): oral glucose if alert; IV dextrose / IM glucagon if not. Commonest from insulin/sulfonylureas.

Type 1 vs Type 2

Type 1 young/lean, abrupt, ketosis-prone, autoantibodies+, C-peptide low; insulin always
Type 2 older/obese, insidious, acanthosis nigricans, strong FHx, C-peptide normal/high early
LADA adult, autoantibody+, progresses to insulin
MODY young, non-obese, AD inheritance, non-insulin-dependent

Pharmacotherapy by class

ClassBenefit / caution
Metformin1st-line; weight-neutral, no hypo; GI/B12 def; stop eGFR<30, hold for contrast/acute illness
SGLT2i (-flozin)CV + HF + renal protection, weight/BP ↓; euglycemic DKA, genital mycotic, volume depletion — hold when sick
GLP-1 RA (-tide)weight loss + CV benefit; nausea; avoid in personal/family medullary thyroid/MEN2
DPP-4i (-gliptin)weight-neutral, modest; linagliptin renal-safe; don't combine with GLP-1
Sulfonylureapotent, cheap; hypoglycemia + weight gain (glyburide worst in elderly/renal)
TZD (pioglitazone)insulin sensitizer; weight gain, edema/HF, fractures — avoid in heart failure
Insulinbasal ± bolus; hypoglycemia, weight gain
Add to metformin for CVD/HF/CKD → SGLT2i; ASCVD/obesity → GLP-1 RA (organ-protection regardless of A1c)

Microvascular complications

Retinopathy NPDR (microaneurysms, dot-blot hemorrhages, cotton-wool, hard exudates) → PDR (neovascularization → vitreous hemorrhage/detachment); macular edema. Annual screen; anti-VEGF / laser
Nephropathy hyperfiltration → microalbuminuria (confirm UACR ×3, exclude transient) → overt; #1 cause of ESRD; ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i
Neuropathy distal symmetric (stocking-glove) — monofilament screen; autonomic (gastroparesis, orthostasis, ED, silent MI); foot care

Macrovascular & vascular protection

Statin all DM ≥40 or with vascular disease; LDL <2.0 / ≥50% reduction
Blood pressure target ~<130/80; ACEi/ARB if albuminuria or HTN
Antiplatelet ASA for secondary prevention (established ASCVD)
Organ-protective agents SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA for CV-renal risk reduction
Lifestyle smoking cessation, weight loss, exercise, foot/eye/renal screening

Pitfalls

Euglycemic DKA SGLT2i — gap + ketones with near-normal glucose; check ketones if acidotic on a flozin
Don't stop insulin in DKA when glucose normalizes — add dextrose, treat to gap closure
K⁺ before insulin if K<3.3 in DKA
Single UACR never diagnoses nephropathy — confirm and exclude UTI/fever/exercise
Sick-day hold metformin + SGLT2i during acute illness/dehydration
Topic 04 / Diabetes (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 05 Pituitary
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 05 ◇

Pituitary

Endo II · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
Physiology & the anatomy that gets tested
Anterior (adenohypophysis): FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH, Prolactin, GH — driven by hypothalamic releasing hormones via the portal system. Dopamine tonically INHIBITS prolactin (the only one under inhibitory control).
Posterior (neurohypophysis): stores hypothalamic ADH & oxytocin (no synthesis there).
Mass effect: upward growth compresses the optic chiasm → bitemporal hemianopsia; lateral → cavernous sinus (CN III/IV/VI). Headache.
Order of hormone loss in compression ("Go Look For The Adenoma… Promptly"): GH → gonadotropins (LH/FSH) → TSH → ACTH; prolactin often rises (stalk effect).
Stalk effect: compression cuts dopamine delivery → mild prolactin rise (<100–150). Hook effect: a very high prolactin reads falsely low unless the sample is diluted.
★ Pituitary emergency
Pituitary apoplexy — sudden hemorrhage/infarction of a (usually pre-existing) adenoma: thunderclap headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia, ↓consciousness. Precipitants: pregnancy/postpartum (Sheehan), anticoagulation, surgery.
The killer is acute secondary adrenal insufficiency. Give IV hydrocortisone immediately, resuscitate, urgent MRI + neurosurgery/ophthalmology; consider surgical decompression.

Prolactinoma / hyperprolactinemia

Presentation women: galactorrhea, oligo/amenorrhea, infertility; men: ↓libido, ED, mass effects (present later/larger)
Confirm prolactinoma prolactin often >250 µg/L; rule out the mimics first
Mimics (mild ↑) pregnancy, dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics, metoclopramide), hypothyroidism (↑TRH), renal failure, chest wall, stalk effect
Treat dopamine agonist (cabergoline > bromocriptine) — shrinks even macroadenomas; surgery only if resistant / progressive visual loss / CSF leak

Acromegaly

Cause GH-secreting adenoma (gigantism if before epiphyseal closure)
Features acral enlargement (shoe/ring/jaw — prognathism), macroglossia, sweating, carpal tunnel, OSA, HTN, T2DM, cardiomyopathy, colon polyps
Work-up
Screen IGF-1 (GH is pulsatile)
Confirm OGTT — GH fails to suppress <1 µg/L
Then pituitary MRI
Treat transsphenoidal surgery 1st; somatostatin analogs (octreotide/lanreotide), pegvisomant (GHR antagonist), cabergoline. Mortality is cardiovascular.

Hypopituitarism

Causes adenoma/treatment, Sheehan (postpartum infarction — failure to lactate, amenorrhea, fatigue), apoplexy, trauma, radiation, infiltration (sarcoid, hemochromatosis, LCH), lymphocytic hypophysitis (checkpoint inhibitors), empty sella
Diagnose low target hormone + low/inappropriately normal trophic hormone; dynamic testing; MRI
Replacement — order matters
Glucocorticoid FIRST then levothyroxine — thyroxine first can precipitate adrenal crisis
Then sex steroids, GH as needed; follow free T4 not TSH (TSH unreliable in central disease)

Posterior pituitary — Diabetes insipidus

Picture polyuria + polydipsia, dilute urine, hypernatremia if water access limited
Central ↓ADH — trauma, surgery, tumor, infiltrative
Nephrogenic ADH resistance — lithium, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, hereditary
Water deprivation test urine fails to concentrate → give desmopressin: central concentrates, nephrogenic doesn't
Treat central → desmopressin; nephrogenic → treat cause, thiazide, low-salt/protein (± amiloride for lithium)
(SIADH — the opposite; covered under Fluids & Electrolytes)

Other masses & axes

Cushing disease ACTH adenoma — see Adrenal reference (high-dose dex suppresses; IPSS)
Nonfunctioning adenoma commonest macroadenoma; presents with mass effect/hypopituitarism
TSH-secreting adenoma ↑TSH + ↑fT4 (inappropriate) — rare
Craniopharyngioma child/young adult, suprasellar, calcified, bitemporal hemianopsia + DI
Incidentaloma screen for hyper-/hypo-secretion + visual fields if near chiasm

Pitfalls

Central thyroid/adrenal don't trust TSH/normal ACTH — measure free T4 and do dynamic cortisol testing
Hook effect huge prolactinoma with "normal" prolactin — dilute the sample
Always exclude pregnancy before chasing hyperprolactinemia/amenorrhea
Cortisol before thyroxine in any panhypopituitarism replacement
Topic 05 / Pituitary (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 06 Reproductive Gonadal GAHT
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 06 ◇

Reproductive / Gonadal & GAHT

Endo III · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
HPG axis & the rule that explains the labs
Axis: hypothalamic GnRH (pulsatile) → pituitary LH/FSH → gonads (testosterone/estradiol, inhibin). Pulsatile stimulates; continuous SUPPRESSES — basis of GnRH-agonist downregulation (puberty blockers, prostate Ca).
Localize by gonadotropins: gonad failing → low feedback → ↑LH/FSH (hypergonadotropic = primary). Pituitary/hypothalamic failure → ↓/normal LH/FSH (hypogonadotropic = secondary).
Menstrual cycle: follicular (FSH → estradiol rises) → LH surge → ovulation → luteal (corpus luteum → progesterone; day-21 progesterone confirms ovulation).
Androgen sources (women): ovary + adrenal. ↑↑DHEAS → adrenal source; ↑↑testosterone with normal DHEAS → ovarian.
★ Hypogonadism — read LH/FSH to localize
PatternLH/FSHExamples
Primary (hypergonadotropic)↑♂ Klinefelter (47,XXY), cryptorchidism, orchitis, chemo/RT; ♀ Turner (45,X), premature ovarian insufficiency
Secondary (hypogonadotropic)↓ / normalKallmann (anosmia), hyperprolactinemia, pituitary disease, functional hypothalamic amenorrhea, opioids, obesity, exogenous steroids

PCOS & hyperandrogenism (♀)

Rotterdam (2 of 3) oligo/anovulation · clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism · polycystic ovaries on US
Diagnosis of exclusion check TSH, prolactin, 17-OHP (NCAH), Cushing if features, testosterone/DHEAS if rapid/severe (tumor)
Associations ↑LH:FSH, insulin resistance, metabolic syndrome; endometrial hyperplasia/cancer from unopposed estrogen
Treat lifestyle/weight; OCP (cycle control, hirsutism, endometrial protection) ± spironolactone; metformin (metabolic); letrozole 1st-line for ovulation induction
Red flag → think tumour, not PCOS
Rapid virilization very high T (ovarian) or very high DHEAS (adrenal), clitoromegaly, voice change → image, don't label PCOS

Amenorrhea (♀)

Definitions primary: no menarche by 15 (with 2° sexual chars) or 13 (without); secondary: ≥3 missed cycles
Step 1 always pregnancy test; then FSH/LH, prolactin, TSH, estradiol
↑FSH/LH primary ovarian — Turner, POI, gonadal dysgenesis
↓FSH/LH functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (stress/exercise/low energy availability), hyperprolactinemia, Kallmann, pituitary
Anatomic/outflow imperforate hymen, Müllerian agenesis (MRKH), Asherman, CAIS

Male hypogonadism & infertility

Symptoms ↓libido, ED, fatigue, ↓muscle/energy, gynecomastia, infertility, osteoporosis
Confirm morning total testosterone ×2 → then LH/FSH to localize; add prolactin, iron studies (hemochromatosis)
Klinefelter tall, small firm testes, gynecomastia, ↑LH/FSH, ↓T, azoospermia
Semen analysis volume/concentration/motility/morphology; severe oligo/azoospermia → karyotype, Y-microdeletion, CFTR (CBAVD)
⚠ TRT suppresses spermatogenesis — never for fertility; use gonadotropins/clomiphene instead. Also causes polycythemia.

Differences of sex development

5α-reductase deficiency 46,XY; ↓DHT → male internal, undervirilized external; virilize at puberty
CAIS 46,XY; androgen-receptor defect → female phenotype, testes, NO uterus (MIF intact), primary amenorrhea, scant pubic hair
CAH (21-OH) 46,XX virilization / ambiguous genitalia; ↑17-OHP (see Adrenal)
Turner 45,X short stature, webbed neck, streak gonads, coarctation, primary amenorrhea

Menopause & hormone therapy

Labs/picture ↑FSH, ↓estradiol; vasomotor symptoms, genitourinary syndrome, bone loss
HRT — who symptomatic, age <60 or <10 yr from menopause
Add a progestogen if uterus intact (prevents endometrial hyperplasia/cancer)
Transdermal preferred if HTN, VTE risk, migraine (avoids hepatic first-pass)
Vaginal estrogen for genitourinary symptoms only
Contraindications breast cancer, VTE/CAD, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding

GAHT & pitfalls

Gender-affirming hormone therapy
Feminizing estradiol (transdermal preferred) + antiandrogen: spironolactone (→ hyperkalemia) or GnRH agonist; cyproterone (→ ↑prolactin/hepatotox). Monitor estradiol, T, K⁺, prolactin; VTE risk.
Masculinizing testosterone → monitor hematocrit (polycythemia), lipids, BP; amenorrhea expected
Puberty blockers GnRH agonists (continuous → downregulation)
Counsel fertility before starting (gamete preservation)
Pitfalls
Pulsatile vs continuous GnRH continuous = suppression (a frequent trick)
Pregnancy first in any amenorrhea work-up
Spironolactone teratogenic + hyperkalemia — counsel contraception
Topic 06 / Reproductive, Gonadal & GAHT (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only
